grammar.title: ‘Por tanto, si…’
grammar.detail: ‘Therefore, if…’ — a long protasis (the ‘if’ clause) with no apodosis (‘then’ clause) until v.2. Paul stacks four parallel ‘algún…’ phrases before the main command lands.

grammar.title: ‘algún’ before m. singular nouns
grammar.detail: ‘algún estímulo’, ‘algún consuelo’ — algún is the apocopated form of alguno before a masculine singular noun. Means ‘some / any’.

expression.phrase: si sienten algún estímulo en su unión con Cristo
expression.note: Paul uses a rhetorical pileup: he assumes the answer to every ‘if’ is ‘yes’. Spanish reads as a single, rolling sentence.

grammar.title: Imperative + pronoun ‘llénenme’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of llenar + the object pronoun me. Pronouns attach to the end of affirmative commands and require a written accent (lle-NEN-me).

grammar.title: Gerund ‘teniendo’
grammar.detail: ‘teniendo un mismo parecer…’ — present participle (gerundio) expresses the means / manner: ‘by having…’

expression.note: ‘united in soul and thought’ — a strong unity image. NVI uses ‘alma’ (soul) where some Greek lexicons read ‘ψυχή’ as ‘inner life’.

grammar.title: Negative imperative ‘No hagan nada’
grammar.detail: Negative ustedes commands use the present subjunctive: hacer → no hagan. Double negative ‘no… nada’ is required in Spanish.

grammar.title: ‘más bien’
grammar.detail: ‘rather / instead’ — connects a contrast. Common in Spanish argumentative writing.

expression.phrase: consideren a los demás como superiores a ustedes mismos
expression.note: The hinge of Phil 2 — preparing for the Christ-hymn in vv.5–11. ‘consideren’ = active mental choice, not a feeling.
